## reference · ux f-pattern scanning layout

* The F-shaped scanning pattern is characterized by:
  + Horizontal movement across the upper part of the content area (top bar of F)
  + Second horizontal movement down the page, covering a shorter area (lower bar of F)
  + Vertical movement scanning the content's left side (stem of F)
* Implications:
  + First lines of text receive more gazes than subsequent lines
  + First few words on the left of each line receive more fixations than subsequent words
* The F-shaped pattern:
  + Applies to the content area of a web page, not navigation bars
  + Is not a perfect F, but rather a pattern that resembles the shape of F
  + Is alive and well on both desktop and mobile devices
* Other scanning patterns:
  + Layer-cake pattern (scanning headings and subheadings)
  + Spotted pattern (scanning for specific information)
  + Marking pattern (focusing on one place while scrolling)
  + Bypassing pattern (skipping first words of lines)
  + Commitment pattern (fixating on almost everything on the page)
* The F-shaped pattern occurs when:
  + A page has unformatted text
  + The user is trying to be efficient
  + The user is not highly interested or committed to reading
* The F-shaped pattern is bad for users and businesses because:
  + Users miss important content
  + Skipped phrases and words can be crucial
* Good design can prevent F-shaped scanning by:
  + Using proper formatting (bolding, bullets, subheadings)

Sources: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/f-shaped-pattern-reading-web-content/
